comments.content:
I have a friend how I've known for several years who does the SoP with rather nontraditional deities, with a pantheon he more or less invented with however much help from the spiritual realms he may have received. Increasingly, I've seen this person's imbalances ratchet up, becoming more over the top, while his self-awareness around what he is doing has, in seemingly direct proportion, plummeted. At this point he reminds me of a partner who I once dated briefly who practiced magic in a totally unsystematic way and became increasingly unhinged, demanding her partners treat her like royalty as part of her 'kink'.

With said partner I urged her to take up a systematic study and practice of magic. Then she got nasty towards me and I broke it off. While this person has kept it pretty even-keeled with me, nonetheless I feel that I'm watching with increasing horror as this person's character becomes increasingly distorted and degraded.

I've been close to three people that I knew "before and after" who I've seen work spiritual practices to amp up their own imbalances and it is frankly scary. Weirdly, one of these people went by the book and it's a book other people I know have worked with it in what seem like totally balanced and healthy ways. All of these people seemed to have a need for others to treat them as the 'smartest guy in the room,' and have others recognize their power & authority, and have had a certain ruthlessness about it.

Do you have a sense of why there is this apparent recurring pattern of a certain type of personality getting seriously imbalanced with spiritual practices? Is this a problem of only being able to will one thing at a time, and folks using magic to inflate their ego? Any general thoughts on this disturbing issue of folks working spirituality towards personal imbalance? Many thanks, as always, for hosting Magic Monday!

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Magic can really mess you over if you approach it as a way to feed your ego. That's especially likely if you come up with your own pantheon (which will reflect your own imbalances back at you) or invent your own system (ditto), but it can happen even if you don't do this. This is why, until the mid-20th century, it was a firm rule not to let technical discussions of magic get into the hands of the public -- you're going to have some people crash and burn. There are ways to prevent it, but you have to go into the work realizing that magic is not about self-glorification -- and if you've got someone with the kind of ego brittleness you've described, that's not going to be an option.

replies.title: Imbalanced elements
replies.content:
Not JMG but if I may toss my 2 cents in...

This is one reason why so many monasteries, ashrams, temples - and it seems even 19th-early 20th century N. American magic lodges(!) - insist(ed) their disciples do charitable service works - often in quite menial positions - as a condition for continuing receiving teachings and training.

In terms of the elements - if you had to make a guess - which element would you assign to his behavior?

There is an exercise in the first Franz Bardon book, called (I think) Black and White Mirrors. You record your daily activities in a journal in 2 columns. On one side write the activity then on the other write down one of the 5 elements you think most likely represents it. Do this with all activities - positive or negative. Bardon says over time this kind of log will give you an idea of the most-afflicted element within your body and thus an idea of what needs being worked on the most.

Do you think your friend would be amenable to taking up this type of practice?

replies.content:
There are four paths to enlightenment because there are four underlying energies or alignments in life. Everything in existence is on one of those paths, including each of us. Each of those four alignments has its own gifts and weaknesses. Each can manifest as both balancing and imbalancing traits in life and in each of us.

When the imbalancing tendencies overwhelm the balancing ones, each of the four energies end up expressing that imbalance through their preferred pathologies. In humans, egomania, schizophrenia, addiction, depression, obsession, and psychopathy are but a few of the myriad ways the four paths express imbalance.

Individuals on any of the four paths can find themselves further imbalanced through spiritual practice if they are insufficiently ready for the increase in energy that magic causes. If your energy is too far "off", increasing it will just make you even more "off". Each of the four paths go off in their own way and each goes more off into pathology in its own way.

My best guess would be that your friend is on the path of the sage, with its acquisitive and isolating tendencies. When imbalanced, those tendencies can easily manifest as showing off, bragging, and ego inflation. The energy of the sage is very powerful, and is the key to unlocking true balancing of all the energies. The sage's key is best known by its common name: contemplation.

Don't worry about your friend overly much — help when you can, and back away when you can't. We each have to pass through many wonderful and terrible incarnations to learn every possible way not to balance. Eventually, almost everyone, after discovering that resistance is always futile, ends up surrendering to balance. This won't be her happiest incarnation, but she has many more to come to get the knack of it.

— Christophe

I was able to talk to the rapist and I felt a strong benevolent presence around me during the 2 or 3 hours of the event. Afterwards, I took a long shower and had the benefit of a healer who cleaned and cleared me energetically and helped ground me back in my body. It took about a year to get over certain sounds (the loud knocking on a door was one), but I think as long as a person gets grounded after a rape, the leaving the body helps the person to not get lost in the emotion and physical trauma of a rape. I remember telling the prosecutor at the trial that although the rapist may have hurt my body, he didn't touch my soul.

I do think surviving a trauma of an attack intact depends on the person's age, life experiences, connection to the attacker, spiritual life and family or friend support system. It's not a one size fits all.

Yet, if you had asked me a year or two later I would have said everything was fine. Apparently, thinking you are fine a year down the road and not being fine 10 or 20 years later isn't uncommon. Even after ~35 years and some very helpful therapy I still have certain scent triggers. They are fading but still not gone. Not sure how to prepare someone to remain present in those circumstances or if presence helps etherically and astrally.

As far as the non-physical planes, the etheric experiences I had about 10 years later were rather dramatic. I do wonder if some of that was due to openings in my feminine etheric body from trauma. I am also wondering about the impact on my masculine astral body.

Feels like more understanding is coming to me.

Eric

comments.title: Mediumship
comments.content:
Hi JMG,

I'm watching a Netflix documentary series called Surviving Death that is about life after death. (For those who watch documentaries, I recommend the show, it's intriguing.) I'm in a part of the series where they are talking about physical mediumship. Apparently, there are groups of people who still do seances in much the same style as 19th century Spiritualists. They are able to transverse the planes and get physical results such as apparitions popping into the room and ectoplasms. Usually the point of the seances is to contact dead loved ones.

From what little I know of approximately four years of intensively studying the occult, physical mediumship strikes me as a very bad idea. Though the mediums featured on the show seem to know what they are doing, the distinct impression I get is that they are playing with fire. They are doing the seances in a church setting but there does not seem to be any form of banishing rituals before or afterward. Does this sort of thing raise any alarm bells in your sage opinion or am I just being a paranoid doofus?

replies.title: Re: Mediumship
replies.content:
Traditional occultists tended to be very leery of trance mediumship, partly because it's done without magical protections, and partly because the habit of handing over your body to dead people is bad for astral, etheric, and psychic health. Some of the old occult schools took it to the point of forbidding mediums from becoming members.

Keep in mind also that fraud is pandemic in what Dashiell Hammett called "the spook racket." M. Lamar Keene's fine book The Psychic Mafia is well worth reading in this context.

replies.title: Re: Mediumship
replies.content:
Thanks! I'm able to communicate with various non-corporeal entities. As far as my own dead loved ones go, I take a hands-off approach once they are past the Second Death because I don't want to disrupt their post-death experience. The only reason I know about the Second Death and the process at all is because of you and Dion Fortune, especially her book Through The Gates of Death. All my life I was plagued by this form of sensitivity until my recent study of the occult, when it turned into more of a blessing -- I am some type of medium, but I'll never do a seance on purpose. If the dead want to talk to me, I'm there for them, but that is up to them. I live by a graveyard and I talk to the dead people who wander by by house sometimes.

From what I can glean, the group doing physical mediumship workshops in the series isn't fraudulent. Nevertheless, yeah, they are asking for a dead person to possess the medium's body while the medium is in a trance. Ick.

Looking forward to reading The Psychic Mafia. You're the best. Thanks again.

replies.title: Re: Mediumship
replies.content: There's a world of difference between being a clairvoyant and being a medium. A clairvoyant remains conscious and self-aware while experiencing the inner planes, while a medium goes into trance and usually doesn't remember a thing afterwards. Many of the old occult schools encouraged the development of clairvoyance, and some taught specific methods for doing so, because that's both safe and useful. So if you happen to be able to perceive disembodied beings while remaining fully conscious, people like George Winslow Plummer and Manly P. Hall would have nodded encouragingly.

replies.title: Re: Mediumship
replies.content: Thanks for the clarification.  That makes sense.  I have garden-variety clairvoyance.  I will try to get better at it.  I'm wide awake when I perceive disembodied beings.  I don't have too much trouble perceiving certain aspects of the inner planes: let's just say the visualization part of the Sphere of Protection isn't anywhere near the hardest thing about it.  Staying totally focused the whole time, on the other hand...

replies.title: Re: Mediumship
replies.content:
I'll second our host's recommendation for The Psychic Mafia. (M Lamar Keene is pseudonym, adopted by the author for excellent reasons. There's lots of money to be gotten in the spook racket, and much is at stake in keeping it going.)

Note, in particular, Keene's very useful distinction between eyes-open mediums and eyes-shut mediums, i.e., between those fraudulent mediums who know quite well what trickery they are pulling and those who do not. (In theory, there may also be a few genuine professional mediums out there, but I have never heard of one who could convince me.)

replies.title: Re: Mediumship
replies.content: I've met mediums who I think were quite sincere, but none of them did or claimed to do phenomena; they simply went into trance, and various critters spoke through them. None of them made much money, for that matter!

replies.title: Re: Mediumship
replies.content:
That's good to know! I, too, have occasionally experienced various beings speaking through entranced people to very good effect. None of these entranced people were professionals, or thought of themselves as mediums, so that's a limit on the range of my own experiences.

However, pretty much all the eyes-shut professional mediums are quite sincere. I've always tried to keep in mind the old con-artist's maxim: "Sincerity is key. If you can fake sincerity, you can do almost anything with almost anyone. (There are definitely "unconscioius con-artists" out there, perhaps in even greater numbers than conscious ones.)

replies.title: Re: Mediumship
replies.content: One has to ask if they actually believe anything happens when they go into a trance. Because if they do believe, it's a heck of a lot like allowing a bunch of creepy guys to spike your drink at a frat party and trusting them not to do anything to you while you're asleep.

comments.title: Casting out demons
comments.content:
This past Monday I unwittingly performed my first exorcism. Both I and my possessed friend were a bit surprised when I named the demon, called it forth, embodied it, and then reflected it back at itself. In my friend, the demon went from its usual raging, tantrum-throwing self to trying to hide behind an agreeable mask of obsequious civility, once I started channeling it.

There was no way I was going to let it stay in residence behind that mask, so I taunted it — using its own voice and power — for cowering in fear when it saw that it had no control over me. After all those years of working my friend like a puppet, the demon saw itself getting manipulated like a puppet by someone else. It was absolutely terrified, and I could feel its fear as I made it bark orders and cruelly describe all its weakness back at itself.

I don't know if I somehow ended up frightening the demon away, or if I just revealed to my friend how powerless it actually was, providing him with the confidence he needed to chase it out himself. It was a ridiculously powerful process with huge amounts of energy moving through both of us over the next few days. It was also amazingly successful with all his raging and tantruming resolved. Amazingly, neither of us has noticed any imbalance or misgivings about the completely unexpected experience.

So, my question is what cleansings or protections are advisable after an exorcism? For my dispossessed friend, to ensure that the demon can't return and that he can heal and strengthen unmolested. For me, to make sure that having allowed the demon to possess me hasn't left an opening for that or another demon to return.

Also, now that I've successfully exorcised a demon, do you have any idea how other demons will relate to me? Will they know what I've done? Will they cower in fear? Will they seek me out? Will they want to test their mettle against me? Lord, I do hope not! I don't really want this to become any kind of regular occurrence in my life.

— Christophe

replies.title: Re: Casting out demons
replies.content:
1) Daily banishings and daily hoodoo baths for a week ought to take care of any lingering astral crap.

2) Good question. What happened the couple of times I've messed with demonic entities is that they hightailed it out of the vicinity and I didn't have any further contact with them or their equivalents for a good long time. Of course I was using different methods -- my standard practice is to break out the Golden Dawn toolkit, open in full ritual form, perform the Bornless One ritual at high intensity, and then command the critters. Since your technique was different, your mileage may vary.

replies.title: Re: Casting out demons
replies.content:

Also, regarding my former question about whether pool water is cleansing or too full of other people's junk, it turns out it is very cleansing, being chock full of salts.

— Christophe

replies.content: In my own quite limited expoerience, other demons will avoid you like the plague once you've driven one of them away.  So that's one data-point.  Others' mileage may vary, as always.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
@ open space & Lady Cutekitten:

It's the mystic's path as a family man (or woman), fully engaged in relations with one's spouse and one's own age-mates, raising one's children well, caring lovingly for one's elders, etc., while living in constant awe of realities far beyond one's own very limited comprehension as a human animal. It's not for everyone, not by a long shot; but then, no mystic path is for everyone.

Something of the same sort--those who known better, please correct me if I've gotten it wrong--seems to be recognized in Indian spiritual traditions, where the life of a sage withdrawn into the forest from the world is best reserved for those older people who have already fulfilled their responsibilities to their family in the world.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
From previous discussions: Path of the Hearthfire = Raising kids, maintaining the household, taking care of people, keeping the home-fires burning, generosity, humility, hospitality, and a regular practice of prayer.

In the Orthodox tradition, we have a very similar concept: monks are sort of like the famous athletes of asceticism and mysticism, and they get the most written about them. But it is often said that family life is its own, equal path of spiritual growth and ascesis, and not to be thought of as inferior to monastic life.

replies.title: Re: Multivitamins
replies.content:
I take cheap off-brand multivitamins from the dollar store a block away from my house. What I look for is simply the full standard set of vitamins and minerals, which under US law you have to have to include if you use the label "multivitamin."

In my experience, here as in most other things, the only difference between the cheap stuff sold to the wage class and the expensive stuff sold to the salary class is marketing. (They're often the same products in different packaging.) Since I go out of my way to avoid salary-class privilege signals, I just get the cheap stuff, and have more money for other purposes.

replies.title: Re: Multivitamins
replies.content:
If I may? I worked in the natural health industry in Canada for decades selling vitamins and natural remedies. When you are looking at vitamins check the label for two things. First the vitamins and minerals: make sure they have decent levels of what you're buying. Second the additives: check to see what else is in them in the way of fillers and random chemicals. I know the laws are different here so it may not be explicit on the label.
The third way to check them is to stand loosely with your feet shoulder width apart and your knees unlocked; hold the bottle up to your solar plexus and see if you sway either forward or backward. If it's backward try a different brand.

comments.title: Energy manifestation in meditation
comments.content:
Good morning, JMG! I’m hoping that you might offer some insight into a development in my meditation practice.

Being called to a more mystic than occult path, I’ve been following my morning banishing ritual with a session of meditative prayer rather than discursive meditation. Using prayer beads, I mentally recite the Mercy Prayer in harmony with my breathing.

Unfortunately, chronic pain and fatigue keep me from meditating effectively in an upright and unsupported posture, so I had to resign myself to meditating in a reclined position. I did try the more traditional “corpse pose” for supine meditation, but it made me feel… well, like a corpse. Utterly disconnected and unpleasantly hollow.

Since a large part of my practice focuses on resacralizing embodiment - trying to pray and worship *with* my body, and not from some spaced-out mental realm - this was counterproductive. But I remembered your advice from the DH about keeping the soles of your feet in contact with the ground, so on a whim I tried bending my knees so that I could plant my feet flat even while reclining.

It worked - I immediately felt grounded, and much more connected to what I think of as Christ’s energies in the web of being. So that has been my default meditation posture for some months now. But something slightly odd has begun occurring in the last couple of weeks.

Specifically, I sometimes feel what seems to be some kind of energy pooling around my feet. My meagre psychic abilities tend to manifest tactilely, so the best description I can give is that it feels like reality *thickens* somehow in a sphere around each foot. It’s not uncomfortable or particularly disturbing, just puzzling.

What do you make of this? Should I pay it any mind? (I would ask a mystical practitioner who was well-versed in energy anatomy, but unfortunately I don’t know any, so thank you for indulging my ongoing inquiries as you have.)

-Accidental Mystic

replies.title: Re: Energy manifestation in meditation
replies.content:
If I may chime in -- the posture you are describing (lie on back with knees bent and feet on the floor) is a familiar one to students of yoga. Specifically: if you move the feet slightly wider than the hips and keep the the feet flat on the ground, and rest the knees against each other, and make adjustments until you feel stable, so that no effort is needed to keep the legs in this position -- then you are in a great pose to achieve release of the psoas muscles. I do this pose all the time, it's so relaxing. There is a real danger though: you might fall asleep!

Also, like you, I feel energy pooling in my feet sometimes. When this happens, I like to go to the beach and walk barefoot on the sand. That always seems to balance it out, and it feels so good too!

Best wishes,
Tangerine Crunchy Filbert

The "thickening" sensation tells you that energy is pooling in your feet. Try rubbing the soles vigorously and tapping them with a loose fist after your practice.

comments.title: The teen menace
comments.content:
I find myself increasing concerned about WitchTok (the witchcraft community on TikTok). Not because I’m worried about their stupid antics like cursing the moon, but because they are tiny little ideologues with an oversized platform to bully people of all ages. It’s a large community of people in their teens and early 20s posting (and posing) as experts on witchcraft and the occult and trying to cancel anyone who doesn’t agree with their thoughts on a breadth of topics (most recently I think what they find to be closed vs. open traditions). Personally I’m at the cusp of being too old to really get TikTok, but I worry about what all of this means for the next generation of pagans. Will they grow up and continue to be dangerously self righteous or will there eventually be some kind of natural spiritual correction as they get older? I just think back to my own teenage seeking years and I can’t imagine how wrong my path could have gone if I had taken slightly older teens as experts on all things instead of turning to books written by people who had practiced the craft for decades.

Semi related, I’ve been trying to figure out how culture got so confused in the West lately and I think a big part of it may be the increased sway that teens and early 20 somethings now have thanks to the Internet. It used to be that they could only bully each other and subject parents and teachers to their ideas, but with social media they able to interact directly with everyone from journalists to politicians. comments.title: Anger
comments.content:
Hello,
Thanks for your time and consideration in advance.
Do you have any advice for anger? I’m extremely angry and it feels like this could be funneled magically into something.
Ideally, I would not be angry but years of Buddhist practice have left me with the belief that I will not be rid of it through that method. If anything, it’s grown hotter through denial. It’s a lot of wasted energy I would like to use somehow.

Drb

replies.title: Re: Anger
replies.content:
That's one of the downsides with all the Piscean revealed religions -- they try to get rid of negative emotions by suppressing them, and it never works.

Anger, according to the teachings in which I've been trained, is always a secondary emotion -- it always has its roots in shame, grief, or fear which haven't been accepted and worked through. That's why it's so difficult to get rid of! Journaling is one good way to work with this; have dialogues with your anger, letting it say whatever it wants to say, and see if you can work through it to the primary emotion beneath it, and (in most cases) to the unresolved childhood experiences to which that primary emotion is linked. It may take a while to get there, but it's worth doing.

replies.title: Re: Anger
replies.content: Once the primary emotion is found and linked to the childhood experience that originated it what do you do to fully integrate it?

replies.title: Re: Anger
replies.content: Usually just letting yourself experience it and accept it will do most of what needs to be done. If not, returning to it, letting yourself feel it, and accepting the reality of it at intervals will do it.

replies.title: Re: Anger
replies.content:
Interestingly enough, I was given similar (or perhaps parallel) information by a therapist specializing in religious trauma—a rare breed, as research into religious trauma gets swiftly shot down in psychology departments whenever the question of funding arises, or so I’m told. Anywho, this gentleman told me that his clients who grew up in deeply [piscean] religious homes have often been so well trained in suppressing anger and other negative emotions that they don’t really even have access to their full nervous systems. In situations of great stress, then, they tend to feel overwhelming fatigue, because the only nervous response they have access to is the one that resides in the parts of the brain we evolved first: “playing dead.” His advice to clients isn’t dissimilar to yours: let yourself feel angry. Otherwise, you can’t regain the use of the rest of your nervous system responses...

Best,
Ryan M.

replies.title: Re: Anger
replies.content: Interesting!  That makes a great deal of sense. The material I passed on comes from the Order of Spiritual Alchemy, one of the sister orders of AODA; it's a free mix of New Thought, Freudian and Jungian psychotherapy, and occultism, but it works quite well in practice.

replies.title: Re: Anger
replies.content: Interesting that anger is not considered a primary emotion in this discussion. The approach that I personally found the most helpful when dealing with emotions in myself and other people- enneagram - stipulates that there are 3 primary emotions - anger, shame, and fear. People's personalities are structured to deal with them in 3 ways: face, run away, have a push-and-pull relationship. There are 9 types in total.

Interesting. Yes, that might work; you might also try using the Christian version of the Middle Pillar exercise, translating it into Latin as required.

************
First, perform the complete Banishing Ritual of the Cross.

Second, imagine a ray of light descending from infinite space to form a sphere of light just above your head, around eight inches in diameter. This sphere is white and contains a blood-red cross. When you have formulated it clearly in your imagination, vibrate the word IESOUS three times. (This is the Greek version of Jesus. Give each vowel its full pronounciation: "ee-ay-so-oos.")

Third, bring the ray of light to your throat, and form another sphere. This one is violet and contains a blood-red cross. When you have formulated it, vibrate the word CHRISTOS three times.

Fourth, bring the ray of light to your heart, and form a third sphere, which is brilliant gold like the sun, and contains a blood-red cross. The word THEIOS is vibrated here three times; again, pronounce each vowel -- "thay-ee-oss."

Fifth, bring the ray of light to your genital center, and form a fourth sphere. This one is green and contains a blood-red cross. HUIOS is vibrated here three times -- "hoo-ee-oss."

Sixth, bring the ray of light to your feet, and form a fifth center. This one is black and contains a blood-red cross. The name vibrated here, three times, is SOTER.

(The words, of course, are Greek for "Jesus Christ, Son of God, Savior," and their initials spell out the Greek word for "fish." That sequence was used by Christians to identify themselves to each other in the earliest days of the church, and it identifies the practitioner with the Christians of those days. The five red crosses symbolize the five wounds Christ suffered in the crucifixion.)

Seventh, return the attention to the center at the top of the head. Bring a current of white light a few inches wide down the left side of the head and neck, the left shoulder and arm, and theleft hip and leg, down to the center at the feet, and then back up the right leg and hip, the right arm and shoulder, and the right side of the neck and head, back to the center at the head. Repeat this a total of four times; if possible, synchronize with the breath, so that the energy flows down with the outbreath and up with the inbreath, but it is more important to visualize the whole course than to make it happen within a single breath.

Eighth, in the same way, bring a current of white light from the center above the head, down the midline of the front of the body to the center at the feet, and then back up the midline of the back of the body to the center above the head. Repeat a total of four times.

Ninth, turn the attention to the center at the feet. Breathing in, draw a current of energy up the Middle Pillar of the body from that center to the center above the head; breathing out, allow it to spray like a fountain out and over the whole body, cleansing the entire aura, pooling at the feet and being drawn back up the Middle Pillar with the next inbreath. Do this a total of four times.

Tenth, make the sign of the cross with the usual words. This completes the exercise.

replies.title: Re: Video calls
replies.content:
If I may interject re the physiology of teleconferencing specifically - particularly if there is more than one face at a time to focus on, you'll be asking your eye muscles to do far more work than they're used to, in the middle of also keeping track of the conversations, compensating for not being able to watch body language (if that's something you read), as well as keeping your own role in mind, processing information, etc and so forth. Add to that a little tension from feeling self-conscious, if that's a concern, and it's a recipe for exhaustion. It may seem overly basic a point, but all the muscles are connected - so when you're fatiguing your eye muscles, or your shoulders from being tense, and you're cognitively processing more information than you're used to (or even just processing it differently than you're used to), everything else is in the process of getting exhausted as well. That's not to say there aren't other causes, but it's (IMHO) important not to underestimate the physiological toll of sitting in front of a computer focusing on people saying a bunch of things, and also keeping track of your own stuff as well.

The following is not medical advice; if you continue to have concerns, definitely talk to your doctor.

There are four things I've found particularly helpful with what might be broadly termed 'facial and cervical fatigue.' One is qigong meditation, which you can find in the book "Qigong Empoowerment: Guide to Medical Taoist Buddhist Wushu Energy Cultivation," by Master Shou-Yu Liang and Wen-Ching Wu. Another is what's called (in English) "Chinese Eye Exercises" ... Which is a process of massaging acupuncture points in a specific order. Information on this is widely available via search engine. Along those lines as well, the Qigong 'Eight Pieces of Brocade' series of exercises has been a life-saver, but keep in mind you may want to speak to your doctor first before trying them, and it is energetically important to do the whole series in order, gently and without strain. Finally, if you are open to watching videos, I found this specific video helpful as well. It details a process of self-massage: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=QA-wi0d7-Ro. There may be reasons any or all of these are not suitable for you in your specific situation, so please do mention it to your doctor before trying them if you have concerns.

As an aside, if you do Western ritual magic, you will want to be careful in combining modalities. Personally, I have had no problems with Qigong; this may be because I had acupuncture regularly for years pre-Covid, and in my experience the Qigong meditation functions similarly to acupuncture after several months of practice. I took it up specifically because I've not been able to access acupuncture since last year.

That said, one of the particular good things about the Liang & Wu book is that they have a section at the end that is a guide for "how to solve problems you caused to yourself by overdoing it", in the absence of access to an acupuncturist. Best not to overdo it in the first place though!

comments.title: Ephemeris Time, an answer
comments.content:
This is an informational comment rather than a question. A few weeks ago I enquired about Ephemeris Time versus GMT, as the former is the kind used in the ephemeris I ordered online recently, the Astrolabe Ephemeris. ET is supposed to be more accurate, according to the foreword by Robert Hand. So I've tested it for a few different days and times, using the Sun's position as a benchmark, down to one second of arc, and comparing that with the time shown for my position on Astrodienst. It turns out that to match Ephemeris Time to my time zone (Pacific Standard Time) I subtract eight hours and two minutes from midnight (or noon, in the case of the Moon), rather than eight hours exactly as for GMT. When Daylight Savings Time comes, it will be seven hours and two minutes' time difference. Such is astronomical super-accuracy. Whoop-de-do.

There is also information that I consider superfluous, viz., the positions of several asteroids including Chiron. Their absence from the book would reduce its rather hefty bulk and weight by about 40 percent.

Still, it has the critical information I want and I'll use it, having worked out what is the time difference to my location.

Kevin

comments.title: Witch bottles
comments.content:
Hi JMG,

What are your thoughts on witch bottles?

A little over a year ago, I felt that my house had a strangely sad, slightly dark and angry feel about it, and I couldn't shake it, so I found a recipe for making a witch bottle. (I can't remember where, but this was before I discovered the Lucky Mojo website, courtesy of yourself!) It seemed fairly logical and consistent with other traditions such as hoodoo, with white wine vinegar, back yard earth, bent nails, rose thorns, sea salt and some protective herbs. (I didn't add anything of my own though, such as nail clippings or urine, and knew enough to steer well clear of any recipes which called for blood or other... fluids) I filled the bottle, sealed it with a cork, then capped it with black wax, as indicated in the recipe.

I stored it in a drawer for about 6 months and forgot about it, but when I came to move the drawer unit, the bottle had leaked and spilled its contents all over the drawer. (The bottle was vertical still, and the drawer hadn't been opened at all to disturb it - it was the seal on the wax and the cork which had burst) I took this to be quite a bad sign, and put the bottle in the outside bin, then wiped out all the residue from the drawer with some salt and cayenne pepper in some vinegar (fortunately the drawer is laminate, so nothing soaked in), binned that cloth as well, then gave myself a hoodoo wash in the bath.

Nothing catastrophic appeared to follow afterwards and it's now 6 months ago, so I think I got away with it, but I was just wondering if:

a) I interpreted the burst bottle correctly as a bad sign (rather than just the earth or vinegar fermenting, for example)
b) cleaned it up correctly, especially just putting it in the bin
c) whether I'm out of the woods in terms of negative effects, or I should do a few more frequent hoodoo washes to be on the safe side (is there such a thing as too many?)
d) whether it's worth trying again, or that if there is something malevolent, that I'm just more likely to annoy it? If so, is there any other folk, rather than ceremonial magic, which may be more effective?

For reference, I also do an SoP every day, in fact in the same room that the drawer is now in (though it wasn't there when it had the bottle in it).

Thanks for providing this discussion space each week by the way!

Mister Crow

replies.title: Re: Witch bottles

Folk magic methods will often behave like that when they get hit by magic that's too much for them to handle; this is among the reasons why I prefer ceremonial magic. If you decide to try it again, btw, ammonia is a good substitute for urine, and doesn't ferment.

replies.title: Re: Witch bottles

We had a mysterious problem for a while with people being *very* forgetful and putting open wine bottles away in the pantry without their corks! After a few repetitions, we finally smacked our heads and figured out that the bottles were uncorking themselves, because the house was quite a bit warmer during the day, than it had been when we recorked the bottle after dinner in the evening. The air in the bottle expands, and Pop!

comments.title: Honoring Johnny Appleseed Day
comments.content:
In honor of Johnny Appleseed Day on March 11:

People who don’t have room to plant an apple tree might consider planting smaller plants, flowers or herbs, which can grow in your yard or even in large pots filled with soil. In most parts of the country this is a good time to plant bachelor buttons, columbine, calendula, and many more. Grow cilantro, dill and parsley from seed. Grow chives or mint from starts – you can divide a well-grown start and each chunk will grow on its own.

People also might be interested in the Seed Saving Guide published by the Seed Ambassadors Project.
https://www.seedambassadors.org/seed-saving-guide/
It’s easy to save your own seeds from plants like lettuce and peas, because they mostly self-pollinate. It’s more difficult to save seed from cole crops such as broccoli and cauliflower because they cross-pollinate with kale and cabbage. This guide tells you which plant families are easy, and the best ways to save and store your seed.

From EllenZ

replies.title: Re: Developing the astral senses
